title: DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY 106, 166-173 (1984) Adenosine and its Derivatives Inhibit the CAMP Signaling Response in Dictyostelium discoidem ANNE THEIBERTAND~ETER N.DEVREOTES
text:
DEVELOPMENTAL 
BIOLOGY 
106, 166-173 (1984) 
Adenosine and its Derivatives Inhibit the CAMP Signaling 
Response in Dictyostelium discoidem 
ANNE 
THEIBERTAND~ETER 
N.DEVREOTES 
Department 
of Biological 
Chemistry, 
The Johns Hopkins 
University 
School of Medicivw, 
725 Nwrth 
Wolfe Street, Baltimore, 
Maryland 
21205 
In developmentally 
competent 
Dictyostelium 
discoidercm amoebae, binding of CAMP to high-affinity 
surface receptors 
produces a rapid activation 
of adenylate 
cyclase which 
adapts within 
minutes. 
The result 
is a transient 
increase 
in 
intracellular 
CAMP which 
is rapidly 
secreted. 
Adenosine 
inhibited 
this CAMP signaling 
response with 
an apparent 
K, of 300 FM. The apparent 
Ki’s for 2’-O-methyladenosine 
and 2-chloroadenosine 
were approximately 
30 and 100 FM, 
respectively. 
Inhibition 
by adenosine 
was rapid, 
reversible, 
and depended on the CAMP stimulus 
concentration, 
In 
addition, 
the adaptation 
of the CAMP signaling 
response was blocked by adenosine. 
As has been previously 
reported, 
adenosine 
inhibits 
CAMP binding 
to intact 
cells. Under 
the same developmental 
conditions 
as in the perfusion 
studies, we find the binding 
inhibition 
depends on both the CAMP and adenosine 
concentrations, 
with 
an apparent 
Ki of 100 pM. The apparent 
Ki’s for 2’-O-methyl- 
and 2-chloroadenosine 
were approximately 
8 and 35 pM, respectively. 
However, 
with 
cells developed 
for short 
times 
and with 
an axenic 
strain, 
inhibition 
was independent 
of CAMP 
concentration 
or cells showed mixed-type 
binding 
inhibition. 
The effect of adenosine on the CAMP signaling 
response 
is consistent 
with 
the reported 
effects of adenosine 
on other CAMP-mediated 
processes such as chemotaxis 
and the 
increase 
in intracellular 
cGMP, and may provide 
an explanation 
for the reported 
inhibition 
of center 
formation. 
C 1984 Academic 
Press. Inc. 
INTRODUCTION 
In 
Dictyostelium 
discoideum 
starvation 
of single 
amoebae triggers 
a developmental 
program 
which 
ul- 
timately 
leads to the formation 
of two cell types- 
stalk and spore cells. Early in development, 
cell mono- 
layers divide into territories 
of 105-lo6 cells and cells 
within 
the territory 
aggregate 
to the center. Aggre- 
gation 
is mediated 
by extracellular 
adenosine 
3’:5’- 
cyclic 
monophosphate 
(CAMP). 
Periodically 
secreted 
by cells in the center of a territory, 
CAMP diffuses to 
nearby cells and binds to high-affinity 
surface receptors, 
leading 
to transient 
activation 
of adenylate 
cyclase. 
The result 
is a rise in intracellular 
CAMP which 
is 
rapidly 
secreted. Referred 
to as the CAMP signaling 
response, this process is repeated in more distal 
cells 
and produces a coordinated 
cell-to-cell 
relay of CAMP 
in the form of CAMP waves which 
propagate 
through 
the monolayer. 
These waves provide 
gradients 
which 
guide the chemotactically 
sensitive 
cells toward 
the 
central emitting 
source (Devreotes, 1982; Gerisch, 1982; 
Van Haastert 
and Konijn, 
1982). In addition 
to its role 
in early development 
in signal relay and chemotaxis, 
CAMP has been implicated 
in the regulation 
of gene 
expression throughout 
development 
(Lodish et al, 1982) 
and in differentiation 
of the multicellular 
organism 
through 
the slug stage to stalk and spore cells (Suss- 
man, 1982). 
Recently, 
Newell 
and Ross reported 
that adenosine, 
when added to the support 
agar or sprayed on devel- 
opmentally 
competent 
cells could, in a dose-dependent 
manner, decrease the number and increase the size of 
territories 
in a cell monolayer 
(Newell and Ross, 1982). 
Bradley also reported a similar 
effect of adenine (Brad- 
ley et al., 1956). The observation 
by Newell 
and Ross 
suggested that adenosine 
can block center formation 
but does not affect chemotaxis 
or signal 
relay. They 
reported 
that 
adenosine 
inhibits 
up to 90% of the 
binding 
of CAMP to cells, yet observed no inhibition 
of 
the other CAMP-mediated 
responses tested. However, 
Brenner has observed inhibition 
of the CAMP dependent 
rise in CAMP by adenosine (Brenner 
and Thorns, 1984). 
And 
in addition, 
studies 
recently 
reported 
by Van 
Haastert 
indicate 
that adenosine blocks chemotaxis 
to 
CAMP and inhibits 
the rise in cGMP as well as inhibits 
binding 
of CAMP to its receptor 
(Van Haastert, 
1983). 
He concluded 
that these inhibitory 
effects were inde- 
pendent of test stimulus 
concentration, 
consistent 
with 
the observation 
of Newell 
and Ross that 
center for- 
mation was inhibited 
to the same extent by adenosine 
concentrations 
ranging 
from 5 to 20 mM and that the 
amount 
of binding 
inhibition 
did not depend on the 
CAMP concentration 
(Newell 
and Ross, 1982). 
Using a perfusion 
assay to control the CAMP stimulus 
concentrations, 
we have examined 
the effect of adeno- 
sine on the CAMP signaling 
response. We have also 
measured the inhibition 
of CAMP binding 
by adenosine 
and the adenosine analogs 2-chloroadenosine 
and 2’-O- 
methyladenosine. 
We report 
here that adenosine 
and 
these adenosine 
analogs 
rapidly 
inhibit 
the CAMP

T~IEIBERT 
AND 
DEVRE~TES 
CAMP 
Signaling 
Response 
Inhibitim 
167 
dependent 
rise in CAMP. The amount 
of inhibition 
depends on both the concentration 
of adenosine 
and 
the concentration 
of CAMP stimulus. 
Consistent 
with 
this, we have found that adenosine and these analogs 
inhibit 
CAMP binding 
and under developmental 
con- 
ditions 
similar 
to those used in the perfusion 
experi- 
ments, the observed 
binding 
inhibition 
also depends 
on both the adenosine and CAMP concentrations. 
Under 
different 
developmental 
conditions, 
however, 
noncom- 
petitive 
or mixed-type 
inhibition 
was observed. Since 
adenosine 
can inhibit 
CAMP binding 
to its receptor 
(either 
directly 
or indirectly 
through 
an adenosine 
receptor), 
it could inhibit 
processes requiring 
receptor 
occupancy. 
If the amount 
of inhibition 
by adenosine 
depends on the concentration 
of CAMP used to test the 
response, this 
may explain 
the discrepancy 
between 
the sensitivity 
of CAMP-mediated 
processes to adeno- 
sine between that shown here for CAMP signaling 
and 
that 
reported 
by Newell 
and Ross (1982) and Van 
Haastert 
(1983) for chemotaxis 
and cGMP accumula- 
tion. Furthermore, 
inhibition 
of the CAMP signaling 
response may explain 
the inhibitory 
effect of adenosine 
on center formation 
observed in viva 
MATERIALS 
AND 
METHODS 
Perfusion. 
NC-4 cells were grown 
as described 
(De- 
vreotes et al, 1979; Dinauer et al, 1980a). NC-4 amoebae 
were washed off nutrient 
agar plates in DB (10 mM 
phosphate 
buffer 
(Na-K), 
pH 6.6, 2 mM MgSO,, 
200 
PM CaC12) and freed of bacteria by four centrifugations 
(1500g for 4 min) at 4°C. Amoebae were shaken at 5 
X lo6 cells/ml 
(1.5 ml) with 
[3H]adenosine-labeled 
E. 
coli (Devreotes 
et ab, 1979). After 
shaking 
for 3 hr at 
22°C (100 rpm) amoebae were transferred 
to nutrient- 
free agar plates (35 mm VWR polystyrene) 
containing 
3 ml of 2% agar in DB. After 
30 min cells adhered and 
excess fluid was removed, lids were replaced, and cells 
were then 
incubated 
at 7°C for 17 hr in the dark. 
When the first 
signs of aggregation 
appeared, 
cells 
were gently 
washed 
off the plate with 
2 ml of DB. 
Identical 
aliquots 
(200 ~1) were pipetted 
onto eight 
Millipore 
filters 
(SSWP 01300) in the perfusion 
appa- 
ratus. Total radioactivity 
was between 2 and 6 X lo6 
cpm per filter. 
[3H]cAMP 
was purified 
as described 
by 
Solomon (1979). 
CAMP binding. 
The binding 
was assayed using the 
ammonium 
sulfate 
stabilization 
technique 
described 
by Van Haastert 
and Kien (1983). NC-4 amoebae were 
harvested 
off growth 
plates, 
washed 
with 
DB four 
times (15009 for 4 min) at 4°C to free them of bacteria, 
and resuspended 
at 2 X lo7 cells/ml 
in DB. After 
7-9 
hr, cells were washed 
with 
10 mM DTT 
in 10 mM 
phosphate 
buffer 
(Na-K), 
pH 6.6, and resuspended 
in 
this at 2 X 10’ cells/ml. 
The final reaction 
volume was 
100 ~1 and contained 
lo7 cells. After 
binding 
[3H]cAMP 
for 2 min at O”C, 2 ml saturated 
ammonium 
sulfate 
at 
0°C and 100 ~1 Hz0 containing 
1 mg of bovine serum 
albumin 
were added. The tubes were allowed to sit on 
ice for 5-10 min and then spun at 5000g for 10 min. 
The supernatant 
was aspirated 
and pellets were solu- 
bilized 
with 
100 ~1 1% SDS-10 mM Tris, pH 7.5, and 
counted with 3 ml of Liquiscint. 
Analysis 
of inhibition 
data. In order to analyze 
the 
inhibition 
data simply, 
we assumed 
that 
adenosine 
inhibition 
depends only on the concentration 
of inhib- 
itor and the Ki. We are of course aware of the fact 
that adenosine receptors 
have been reported 
on these 
cells 
(Newell, 
1982, and Van 
Haastert, 
1983). Van 
Haastert 
(1983) has proposed a model in which 
aden- 
osine must first bind to an adenosine receptor and this 
complex inhibits 
CAMP binding 
to its receptor. 
How- 
ever, the objective 
of this analysis 
was to determine 
only 
whether 
adenosine 
inhibition 
of signaling 
also 
depended on the stimulus 
concentration 
and obtain 
a 
quantitative 
measurement 
of inhibition. 
Models 
in 
which 
adenosine 
works 
alone or through 
a receptor 
could produce inhibition 
either 
dependent 
or indepen- 
dent of CAMP concentration. 
We are also aware that 
in the strictest 
sense, a Ki can only be defined for a 
system 
in which 
a single 
Kd can be defined. 
For 
signaling, 
we have assumed a single Kd where Kd = K,, 
the concentration 
of CAMP that elicits a half-maximal 
response, even though 
this value was not required 
for 
the calculation 
of Ki. We have also assumed a single 
Kd for the binding 
data analysis. 
Inhibition 
data were 
plotted as the ratio of control 
to inhibited 
response as 
a function 
of adenosine concentration. 
A series of plots 
were generated 
which were linear 
and intercepted 
the 
y axis 
at 1. If 
the inhibition 
was independent 
of 
stimulus 
concentration, 
a single plot would have been 
generated. 
However, 
a series of plots were generated 
and the slope of each line was related 
to the CAMP 
stimulus 
concentration 
by the equations 
given in the 
figure legends. The inverses of these slopes were then 
replotted 
as a function 
of CAMP concentration 
to yield 
the plots shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. 5. 
Matetiak 
[3H]Adenosine 
(20-50 Ci/mmol) 
and r3H]- 
CAMP (27 Ci/mmol) 
were from ICN, Irvine, 
California. 
Adenosine, 
2’-0-methyladenosine, 
2-chloroadenosine, 
and CAMP 
were 
from 
Sigma, 
St. Louis, 
Missouri. 
Ammonium 
sulfate was from BRL, Bethesda, Maryland. 
All other chemicals 
and materials 
were from standard 
sources. 
RESULTS 
Inhibition 
of the CAMP Signaling 
Response 
Typical CAMP signaling 
responses are shown in Figs. 
1A and E. The rate 
of rH]cAMP 
secretion, 
which

FIG. 1. Inhibition 
of CAMP signaling 
response by adenosine. Rates 
of [3H]cAMP 
secretion 
were measured 
as described. 
Application 
of 
CAMP stimuli 
is denoted 
by the dashed 
rectangles. 
The start 
of 
addition 
of adenosine 
is shown by the arrow. 
The stimulus 
concen- 
tration 
in A-D 
is 10 nM CAMP. (A) Control. 
(B) 1 mM Adenosine. 
(C) 2.5 mM Adenosine. 
(D) 5 mM Adenosine. 
The stimulus 
concen- 
tration 
in E-H 
is 100 nM CAMP. (E) Control. 
(F) 5 mM Adenosine. 
(G) 10 mM Adenosine. 
(H) 20 m&f Adenosine. 
reflects the rate of synthesis 
of CAMP in response to a 
CAMP stimulus, 
rose and fell 
with 
a characteristic 
time course. As has been described 
(Devreotes 
et al., 
1979), cells respond to an increment 
in extracellular 
CAMP concentration, 
adapt 
within 
minutes 
to this 
concentration, 
and remain adapted as long as a constant 
stimulus 
is applied. 
The magnitude 
of the response 
depends on the concentration 
of CAMP stimulus 
(10 
nM in 1A and 100 nM in 1E). To assess the effect of 
adenosine on the CAMP signaling 
response, adenosine 
was added 3 min before and during 
the addition 
of the 
CAMP stimulus. 
Figures 
lB-D 
show 
the effects 
of 
adenosine at the lower stimulus 
concentration 
(10 n&f). 
Addition 
of 1 and 2.5 mM 
adenosine 
reduced 
this 
response by 50 and 86%, respectively, 
and 5 mM com- 
pletely 
blocked the response. Figures 
lF-H 
show the 
inhibition 
by adenosine 
at a higher 
stimulus 
concen- 
tration 
(100 nM). Addition 
of 5 mM adenosine inhibited 
this response by only 5% (lF), 
10 and 20 mM caused 
50 and 90% inhibition. 
Note that 
adenosine 
had no 
effect on the time course of the response, but reduced 
the secretion 
rate to the same extent 
at each time 
point. In addition, 
inhibition 
was not due to a delete- 
rious effect on cell viability 
or metabolism 
since the 
inhibition 
was rapidly 
reversible 
(data shown later). 
These initial 
results 
indicated 
that 
the amount 
of 
inhibition 
depended on both the CAMP stimulus 
con- 
centration 
and the adenosine concentration. 
We there- 
fore carried out a more detailed study of this inhibition. 
The inhibition 
of the signaling 
response 
by various 
concentrations 
of adenosine and the adenosine analog 
2’-0-methyladenosine 
was determined 
at many CAMP 
concentrations 
and analyzed 
as described 
under Ma- 
terials 
and Methods. 
Figure 
2A clearly 
shows 
the 
dependence of inhibition 
on stimulus 
concentration. 
A 
Ki for adenosine 
of 300 pM (Fig. 2A) was obtained. 
Since fewer 
experiments 
were performed 
with 
2’-O- 
methyladenosine 
(Fig. 
2B), fewer 
data points 
were 
obtained. 
To generate 
the curve, we drew the line to 
fit most of the points, excluding 
the highest concentra- 
tion. This gives an approximate 
Ki of 30 pM, indicating 
that it is a much stronger 
inhibitor 
than 
adenosine 
and illustrating 
that the inhibition 
by O-methyladen- 
osine also depends on the stimulus 
concentration. 
We 
also determined 
the Ki for the analog 2-chloroadenosine 
to be approximately 
100 pM (data 
not shown). 
In 
addition, 
from 
the replots, 
the CAMP concentration 
that elicits 
a half-maximal 
response was observed to 
be between 2 and 6 nM.

of Ki’s for signaling

for adenosine

[S] which

by the equation

and the z intercept

for CAMP. (A) Adenosine.

Rapid Kinetics 
of Inhibition 
In these experiments, 
cells were pretreated 
with 
adenosine 
for 3 min before addition 
of the stimulus. 
The same amount 
of inhibition 
was observed whether 
the cells were pretreated 
with adenosine for 10 min or 
if it was added at the same time as the CAMP stimulus. 
However, 
a small delay in the onset of inhibition 
at 
the beginning 
of the response 
may not be detected 
when the entire response is measured since the cellular 
secretion 
rate requires 
between 1 and 2 min to reach 
a peak. To determine 
if there was a delay, the effect 
of the time of pretreatment 
with 
adenosine 
on the 
response to a brief application 
of the CAMP stimulus 
was examined. 
The control 
response to 50 nM CAMP 
for 30 see is shown 
in Fig. 3A. Adenosine 
(20 mM) 
completely 
inhibited 
the response whether 
it was added 
at the same time as the CAMP stimulus 
(3B) or if it 
was added 30 set (3C) or 60 see (3D) before 
the 
stimulus. 
This result indicates 
that no pretreatment 
is 
required 
for inhibition 
and adenosine 
was effective 
within 
a 30-set time period. To further 
establish 
the 
rapidity 
of adenosine 
inhibition, 
we tested whether 
addition 
of adenosine had the same effect as removal 
of the stimulus, 
which 
leads to an abrupt 
decline 
in

the secretion 
rate (Dinauer 
et al., 1980a). As shown in 
Fig. 3E, when after 2 min the CAMP stimulus 
(10 nM 
CAMP) was withdrawn, 
the CAMP secretion rate rapidly 
declined. 
When 10 mM adenosine 
was added after 
2 
min (3E), the CAMP secretion 
rate fell to zero with 
approximately 
the same kinetics 
as it did when the 
stimulus 
was removed. In Figs. 3F and G, this experi- 
ment was repeated except that the stimulus 
was inter- 
rupted (by addition of adenosine or removal of stimulus) 
at 1.5 min (3F) and 1 min (3G). These responses fell 
off with the same kinetics whether 
CAMP was removed 
or adenosine was added. 
Inhibition 
of [3H]cAMP 
Binding 
The rapid inhibition 
of the CAMP signaling 
response 
by adenosine suggested that it may act by preventing 
CAMP binding 
to its surface 
receptor. 
Others 
have 
observed 
inhibition 
of CAMP binding 
by adenosine 
(Newell and Ross, 1982; Van Haastert, 
1983). Although 
they reported 
the inhibition 
to be noncompetitive, 
it is 
clear from the physiological 
studies shown above that 
adenosine inhibition 
of the signaling 
response depended 
on the concentration 
of CAMP used to stimulate. 
We 
therefore 
reexamined 
the effect of adenosine on CAMP 
binding 
on cells developed as described under Materials 
and Methods 
(shaking 
at 2 X lo7 cells/ml 
for 7-9 hr). 
In Fig. 4A, the binding 
data are plotted by the method 
of Scatchard 
(1949) and form a typical 
curvilinear 
plot 
that 
is characteristic 
of [3H]cAMP 
binding 
to intact 
cells. Also shown in Fig. 4A is the CAMP binding 
in 
the presence 
of 150 PM adenosine 
and 15 PM O- 
methyladenosine. 
The inhibitors 
decrease the slopes of 
the binding curves while the x intercepts 
are unaffected, 
indicating 
that the amount of inhibition 
by adenosine 
or 0-methyladenosine 
was dependent 
on the concen- 
tration 
of CAMP in the binding 
assay. To show this 
dependence more clearly, binding was assayed at either 
100 or 1 nM [3H]cAMP 
(Fig. 4B), and competed with 
either 
CAMP, adenosine 
or 0-methyladenosine. 
The 
degree of inhibition 
of 1 nM CAMP is greater 
than at 
100 nM 
CAMP 
for 
every 
competitor 
concentration 
tested. The binding 
data from the above experiments 
and others 
were combined, 
analyzed 
similar 
to the 
signaling 
inhibition, 
and are presented 
in Fig. 5. As 
before, we assumed a single Kd and from these plots a 
Ki for binding 
inhibition 
of 100 PM for adenosine and 
8 PM 0-methyladenosine 
was obtained. 
A Ki for 2- 
chloroadenosine 
was also determined 
(data not shown) 
to be 30 PM. A Kd for the CAMP receptor of 30-40 nM 
was observed. To try to discover 
the reason for the 
discrepancy 
between this result 
and previous 
reports, 
we tested binding 
inhibition 
on axenic cells and NC-4 
cells developed 
for short 
times 
(4-5 hr). We clearly

6A shows the control

6B shows that pretreatment 
with 10 mM adenosine for 
9 min had little 
effect on the cellular 
response, indi- 
cating that the effect of the drug is rapidly 
reversible. 
As shown in Fig. 6C, when adenosine 
is added with 
the stimulus, 
the response is blocked. The adenosine 
was withdrawn 
‘7 min after 
the stimulus 
was added 
and replaced by the CAMP alone. As can be seen, the 
cells responded 
normally 
when the inhibitor 
was re- 
moved. These results 
demonstrate 
that adaptation 
as 
well as excitation 
is inhibited 
by adenosine since had 
adaptation 
proceeded in the presence of adenosine, no 
response would have been observed.

MINUTES 
FIG. 6. Reversibility 
of adenosine 
action and effect of adenosine 
on 
adaptation. 
Addition 
and removal 
of 10 mM adenosine 
is shown by 
the arrows. 
The stimulus, 
shown by the dashed rectangle, 
is 10 nM 
CAMP. (A) The response 
to the stimulus. 
(B) The response 
to the 
stimulus 
after 
pretreatment 
with 
adenosine 
for 
9 min. 
(C) The 
response 
to the stimulus 
after 
pretreatment 
with 
adenosine 
for 2 
min then adenosine 
and CAMP for ‘7 min. 
inhibition 
depends 
on both 
the adenosine 
and the 
CAMP stimulus 
concentration. 
2’-0-methyladenosine 
is 
a more effective inhibitor 
of CAMP signaling 
(Ki = 30 
PM) than is adenosine 
(Ki = 300 PM). The inhibition 
by adenosine was shown to be rapid. Less than 30 set 
was required 
to inhibit 
the response 
(Fig. 3A) and 
when adenosine was added during 
a response, it led to 
a decline 
in the CAMP secretion 
rate with 
kinetics 
comparable 
to that caused by removal 
of the stimulus 
(Fig. 3B). The inhibition 
was also rapidly 
reversible 
since cells that 
had been pretreated 
with 
adenosine 
responded 
normally 
when 
adenosine 
was withdrawn 
and stimulus 
was added (Fig. 6B). This suggests that 
the effect of adenosine 
is not due to a loss of cell 
viability 
or a change in the ATP substrate 
pool. In 
addition, 
cells responded normally 
to the CAMP stim- 
ulus after pretreatment 
with both adenosine and CAMP 
(Fig. 6C), indicating 
that adenosine blocks not only the 
process leading 
to activation 
of adenylate 
cyclase but 
also that leading to adaptation. 
These observations 
are in agreement with previously 
reported 
observations 
of the inhibitory 
effect of aden- 
osine on the CAMP accumulation 
in suspension, 
che- 
motaxis, 
cGMP accumulation, 
and center 
formation. 
First, this report of signaling 
inhibition 
measured with 
the perfusion 
technique 
agrees with 
that reported 
by 
Brenner 
and Thorns 
(1984) in which 
they 
observed 
inhibition 
by adenosine 
of the rise in total CAMP in 
stimulated 
cells in suspension. 
Second, Van Haastert 
reported 
inhibition 
of both chemotaxis 
and the rise in 
cGMP under conditions 
of lower test stimulus 
concen- 
trations 
(Van Haastert, 
1983). However, 
he observed 
that attenuation 
of the cGMP response required 
pre- 
treatment 
with adenosine before addition 
of the CAMP 
stimulus, 
whereas we found that no pretreatment 
was 
required 
for signaling 
inhibition 
(Fig. 3). A possible 
explanation 
is that 
since the cGMP 
response 
is so 
rapid (peak lo-20 see) the equilibration 
time of aden- 
osine binding 
may be a factor in the inhibition 
of this 
response. 
Newell 
and Ross observed that 
adenosine 
changed 
the number and size of territories 
of aggregating 
cells, 
an effect they concluded was due to inhibition 
of center 
formation 
(Newell and Ross, 1982). The CAMP signaling 
response is a likely component involved in the formation 
of centers according to theoretical 
models that account 
for pulsatile 
release of CAMP in terms 
of feedback 
loops whose excitability 
can be raised to the point of 
autonomous 
generation 
of CAMP pulses. A prediction 
of these models is that a decrease in sensitivity 
of the 
signaling 
response (signal relay response) could change 
the excitability 
(but only slightly 
the ability 
to relay) 
of the system and thus eliminate 
spontaneous 
pulsing 
(Goldbeter 
and Segal, 1977). Our results 
show that 
adenosine does inhibit 
CAMP signaling, 
probably 
to a 
small degree at physiological 
CAMP wave concentra- 
tions (0.1 to 1 pLM) and 5-10 mM adenosine. This slight 
inhibition 
may be sufficient 
to change the excitability 
of the system. 
Under 
the assay conditions 
used by 
Newell and Ross (1982, saturating 
CAMP test stimuli), 
they observed no decrease in chemotaxis 
or the rise in 
cGMP and also no change in the rate of wave propa- 
gation. 
If adenosine 
inhibition 
does depend on CAMP 
concentration, 
little 
inhibition 
would 
be expected 
at 
those concentrations 
of CAMP and adenosine. 
Indeed, 
we observed only 50% inhibition 
of the signaling 
re- 
sponse by 10 mM adenosine 
when a stimulus 
of 100 
nM CAMP was applied (Fig. 1). 
Consistent 
with previously 
reported results, we have 
shown here that adenosine inhibits 
binding of [3H]cAMP 
to intact cells. In agreement with the results presented 
above for the signaling 
inhibition, 
the binding inhibition 
for cells developed similarly 
also depended on both the 
adenosine and CAMP concentrations 
(Fig. 4). A Ki for 
2’-0-methyladenosine 
of 8 pM and for adenosine of 100 
172 
DEVELOPMENTAL BIOLOGY 
VOLUME 106, 1984 
pM was obtained. 
Both Newell 
and Ross (1982) and 
Van Haastert 
(1983) showed that adenosine 
inhibited 
CAMP binding 
to cells but both of their results indicate 
that 
this 
inhibition 
was noncompetitive. 
A possible 
explanation 
of the discrepancy 
between their 
results 
and ours may lie in the fact that we observed a mixture 
of apparently 
competitive 
and noncompetitive 
inhibition 
in cells that were developed for shorter 
times and in 
an axenically 
grown strain. 
Adenosine 
receptors have been reported in D. discoi- 
deum by Newell 
(1982) and Van Haastert 
(1983). Van 
Haastert 
has proposed that 
adenosine 
first 
binds to 
the adenosine /3 receptor and the adenosine/P 
receptor 
complex 
inhibits 
the binding 
of CAMP to the CAMP 
receptor. This model is consistent with the observations 
that the Ki for binding 
inhibition 
is similar 
to the Kd 
of the p receptor (350 pM) and that 2’-O-methyladen- 
osine is a more effective 
inhibitor 
of signaling 
and 
binding. 
Both competitive 
and noncompetitive 
inhibi- 
tion could be produced in this model. Either 
type could 
also be produced by direct antagonism 
of the receptor. 
Since either 
type of inhibition 
could be observed 
in 
either model, it is not clear whether 
the mechanism 
of 
action 
of adenosine 
is direct 
or via 
an adenosine 
receptor. 
Is there a physiological 
role for adenosine 
during 
Dictyostelium 
development? 
Brackenbury 
reported that 
addition 
of the adenosine 
analog formycin 
B to mi- 
grating 
slugs could change the morphogenetic 
pathway 
(Brackenbury 
et al., 1974). In addition, 
Armant 
showed 
the presence of 5’-nucleotidase 
in prestalk 
cells at the 
junction 
between prestalk 
and prespore cells (Armant 
et al., 1980). In higher 
eukaryotes 
adenine nucleosides 
and nucleotides 
regulate 
numerous 
physiological 
pro- 
cesses via purinergic 
receptors (Burnstock 
and Brown, 
1981). Adenosine 
and its analogs 
display 
prominent 
neurophysiological 
actions (Phillis 
and Wu, 1981) and 
have both stimulatory 
and inhibitory 
effects on ade- 
nylate 
cyclase (Cooper et ah, 1981; van Calker et al, 
1979). Adenosine has also been shown to inhibit 
platelet 
aggregation 
(Haslam and Cusack, 1981), dilate coronary 
blood vessels (Schrader, 
1981), and have locomotor 
(Snyder 
et al., 1981) and cardiac 
depressant 
effects 
(Schrader, 
1981). 
In D. discoideum, 
adenosine 
or its analogs may be 
useful in studies of the CAMP receptor. Are all CAMP- 
dependent 
processes inhibited 
by adenosine 
with 
a 
similar 
Ki ? The answer to this question 
will 
help to 
establish whether 
a single receptor mediates all CAMP- 
dependent 
processes. In addition, 
adenosine 
may aid 
in identification 
of the CAMP receptor 
since it should 
be a specific competitor. 
Finally, 
adenosine 
may also 
be useful in determining 
the requirement 
for CAMP in 
early development, 
in gene expression 
throughout 
de- 
velopment, 
and in morphogenesis 
and differentiation.
